item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Creepy as @#$%. My question is how they convinced a D-class to hold still while they performed brain surgery on him. +1

item.title: Re: Junior Staff Post - WikiWalk Proposal
item.content:encoded:
I've changed this edit, as (a) it conflates public medical / Foundation diagnoses, suggesting that the public might misdiagnose someone as having SCP-1086; and (b) considering the other symptoms of SCP-1086, it's difficult to imagine the Foundation mixing that up with this disease.

Fortunately there is another, better candidate for a cross-link: SCP-1693. I've made the change as follows:

but may be misdiagnosed as Creutzfeldt–Jakob, Huntington's, Parkinson's or Alzheimer's disease (and was initially thought by the Foundation to be SCP-1693)

item.title: Junior Staff Post - WikiWalk Proposal
item.content:encoded:
Edit: timer expired, edits made

The WikiWalk team is proposing edits to this page. All proposed edits will be in a collapsible below this post, and after 7 days if there are no serious objections to the edits, the feedback received will be taken into account as the edits are made. If you have questions not directly related to the article about this project, please pm a member of the WikiWalk Team.

+ show block
– hide block

This is an SCP that causes a brain disease. However, it also says that it is sometimes mistaken for other brain diseases. I propose a crosslink to SCP-1086, another brain disease, for this.

Editing this:

but may be misdiagnosed as Creutzfeldt–Jakob, Huntington's, Parkinson's or Alzheimer's disease.

To this:

but may be misdiagnosed as SCP-1086, Creutzfeldt–Jakob, Huntington's, Parkinson's or Alzheimer's disease.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
A fine name for the SCP as well.

Thanks! It's probably the first thing I've written for the site (with the possible exception of Blank Space) where I knew exactly what I would call it from the word go :)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I've been thinking about this quite a lot, and I've finally deciphered why I didn't end with the first log. I think that the "pops up in someone else's brain" idea, while it has some punch (and clearly has the most punch for some readers), is probably the "expected twist" for this site. It is one obvious direction to take the anomaly, based on a wormhole within a brain, it is horrific but a little bit jokey - it fits very well within the site aesthetic all round (it is exactly how the Series I version of this skip would have ended, I think - maybe I would add that the unfortunate Mr Miller had taken a pill labelled "increase your brain matter").

I wanted this one to zag slightly when the reader expected it to zig. That's why I wanted the brain-to-brain test to be the first - to get it out of the way, and leave the reader a little more in the dark about where the skip was going. And I wanted the ending to be horrible and inexplicable, with essentially no jokiness at all.

It may not be the right answer, but I thought it was worth explaining why I made the writing choice.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Very creative way of putting body horror on to the website. A fine name for the SCP as well. Keep writing if ye can.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I do rather love that image - to the extent that I am tempted to change Wisconsin to Hawaii, which is close to the antipode of Windhoek.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Great work. I liked everything about this, which makes it difficult to add anything useful. However, the only thing that felt would have made this perfect for me, and this may not be an enhancement to anyone else, was to have two of the locations involved be antipodes, like say, Bermuda and Perth. The image of an invisible column of strangespace shooting through the planet (and perhaps the universe) like a cocktail olive on a toothpick was so attractive to me for some reason.
Again, my desire to add something was inspired by how good your premise is.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Nope, so I guess I have some other brain disease.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Can you feel a liquid iron-nickel alloy sloshing around in there when you shake it?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: My head itches.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I liked that the anomaly is a region of space, it adds to the weirdness and elusiveness of the skip IMO. And it's less common than having the anomaly be a disease, or be caused by an object, etc.

And while I do get why some people would dislike not knowing how this area of space was discovered/delineated, I'm personally fine with it. I'm very much an advocate of the idea that a skip (or any piece of writing, really) should always be pared down to its essentials, and I don't see how adding an elaborate and detailed explanation of how this was discovered would add anything to the skip, except perhaps some (unnecessary) verisimilitude. Unless such a discovery log were to contain new information that either added to the mystery or helped explain the skip better (which doesn't appear to be the case here), then it's best to simply not have one.

Let's not forget also that SCPs aren't meant to represent every piece of information the Foundation has on a skip, but rather to provide an overview of it by presenting the most relevant documentation. It's become an accepted practice on the site to omit any information that only exists because it needs to, while not adding anything to the skip. Look at how many articles have no recovery logs, or quote/make references to, non-existent documents. Hell, look at SCP-173.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The only thing that bugs me is the SCP being a "region of space" and not just the disease itself.

IvanPrz, this is a really fair point, and I worried about it when I was writing. For the record, here are the reasons I went with a region of space rather than a disease - I'm not sure that these are wholly convincing as a justification, but they can at least serve as an explanation.

My other options for the cause of the beaks (as I saw them) were (a) an object with an area of effect; (b) a transmissible disease; (c) unexplainable manifestations.

  • I didn't want to use (a), as I felt tying this to an object suggested a man-made source, which seemed unrealistic considering the effects I wanted.
  • I avoided (b) as I feel like there are too many of these on the wiki, and I would have needed to spend wordcount on method of transmission and pathology, which would have wasted time and reader attention.
  • Option (c) was a possibility, but I decided against it, mostly because I had recently written SCP-2365, which also involves the unexplainable manifestation of something dangerous in relation to your head.
  • A region of space has the advantage of being difficult to analyse in a literary sense. You can't guess where I'm going with the story by using genre-savvy (unlike if I made the cause a statue of a turtle, for example) so the results are more unexpected.
  • The region of space also allows me to hint at the "earth's core" ending very early on without giving anything away, by saying "extends below the ground for at least 10m, but its total depth has not been measured".
  • Finally, the region of space also allows me to have this occur anywhere, invisibly. There are clearly at least two other regions of space doing this that have not been discovered (in Italy and Wisconsin) - I think that adds a little fridge horror to the skip.

I take your point about discovery. My headcanon (which I didn't explain in the skip because it felt distracting from the impetus of the story) is this:

  • because this particular region of space is in a suburb of a (small) city, a number of people would have walked through it, but not too many.
  • the resulting spate of brain disorders would have been statistically significant, led to autopsies and discovered the beaks.
  • the Foundation steps in, and determines that the majority of the affected people lived in the relevant suburb. So they go there and run tests to narrow it down - if they think it's an invisible, stationary effect, all they need to do is work in a grid system through the area.
  • because this affects "any organism with a brain", there's no need to expend D Class. They used small animals to start with - perhaps birds - so while there would have been a lot of testing, it wouldn't be a waste of D Class (or be of a type that allowed the kind of explorations covered in the logs).

Still, I admit that it's still a bit unrealistic that they found this random area of space. If people think it's an issue, I could relocate the space to within a single house, as that would make narrowing it down *much* easier. The problem with that is it may put the reader on the wrong track - they'll start to think this is a story about the house (or that the house or its residents are relevant in some way) rather than it being a random location.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I love the disease. I love the fact that the beak is made of something that already exists in the body (keratin) and is formed inside the body by the body itself and then slowly starts ingesting it, or as the title perfectly described it "the brain that ate itself". I also love the part of the beaks being portals to another dimension, and specially the beaks being all connected (The part where someone died by a camera suddenly appearing in their brain is pure gold) I also liked, although I found it a little weird but cool nonetheless, the tunnel that was connected to earths core and how all the team died because of it.

The only thing that bugs me is the SCP being a "region of space" and not just the disease itself. It just doesn't make sense. First of all, how would the foundation made the link of all the victims having walked through that random space? what would have been the first clue to determine the exact location? and even worse, how would they accurately determine the radio? Did they make D class walk close to it and then waited 4 months? That would have required a lot of specimens and A LOT of testing, yet they only opened 3 of them up. Again, it doesn't make sense.

In my opinion, it should have just been the disease and then mention that they're still investigating on the cause or connection, like many other SCPs have done.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
To be clear, it's here.

https://commons.wikimedia.org/wiki/File:Leatherback_turtle_oesophagus.jpg

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
+ Saving Space
- Save Space

So from what I got from the molten metal part was that these beaks make a portal to other beaks that only exist if someone looks through it with a camera, as the metal went through only when the camera was pushed through. So that makes me (the reader) think, "How the hell could one of those beaks been embedded in such a large amount of molten metal?"
My thoughts are some creature from long ago got this infection and passed due to it, the beak stayed after death and by some such or matter made it into a volcano, lava spout or something, and because of being in all that matter it was able to shoot molten metal so fast it killed all the staff in the room


tl;dr this is some [EXPLETIVE]ing good ass writing right here boy. +1

item.title: Re: Admin Post - Open
item.content:encoded: No need to apologise, Speeth. And I do see your point. I have edited the last test log to make clear that not every single Foundation medical employee was killed, and I've adopted/adapted several of your other suggested changes :)

item.title: Re: Admin Post - Open
item.content:encoded:
Thanks for the reminder, Zyn—will be extra sure to give the site rules a reread to avoid any other infractions.
psul, apologies again for jumping on your stuff w/o permission. As I said in my PM, I really think “all Foundation medical personnel” needs to be rephrased.
Cheers to you both for reprimanding me without ripping out too much of my throat :D

item.title: Admin Post - Open
item.content:encoded:
Speeth (and other editors), please note that the Site Rules state:

Editing Other Articles: You may make minor edits for grammar or spelling to other peoples' articles. Please fill out the 'Short description of changes' box. Major edits require permission from the original author or staff. Stylistic and tonal edits are considered major edits.

Stylistic/language edits are major edits that require permission prior to being made. Please request permission before making such edits yourself.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
So it's an extradimensional pot of boiling metal that uses its pet location to send brain-eating beaks into peoples' brains?

This is fantastic.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Hi, author here. I've reverted the draft - while there wasn't much wrong with your changes, there were a few stylistic points ("8 seconds", "instantaneously") that I preferred in the previous version. I'd suggest asking before making that type of change in future - if it's more than just spelling errors, it's always better to ask.

And hey - thanks to everyone who voted on this in the past few days. I had no idea that it was featured until I saw the rating shooting up and wondered why!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Really fantastic medical SCP. +1 for sure.
Made a bit of an edit to the 3rd log; language around the vanished camera and the subsequent molten nickel fountain felt a little vague/weak. If I overdid it, please let me know :)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The use of human test subjects was approved based on the requirements for brain size and ease of anaesthesia.

THANK YOU! Finally someone tried to answer the question "why Foundation always skips animal testing and goes right to D class carnage?"

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Really liking this one - it's short, packs a hell of a punch, and leaves you wanting more. While I see how it is a sequel of sorts to SCP-2365, for some reason I was more reminded of SCP-3512, which is a good thing - maybe it's because that circular space with all the openings reminded me of the subterranean complex in 3512? Or is it the body horror? Or maybe it's just the general unknowable-ness of the skip, combined with the sense that this would all make sense if we were given just a little more information - that somehow there's an underlying logic to it all. That's not to say that this feels derivative of 3512, as it is very much its own thing. But I loved getting to feel some of the same things I felt when reading 3512.

Anyway, excellent work, I could see this article becoming a classic.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Am I the only one to think of earth's core here?

Nope - indeed your instincts could get you a job at the Foundation:

Proposals for a network of Foundation seismographs to detect potential anomalies in the Earth's outer core are currently under consideration.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This last log:

Iron-Nickel-Alloy? 4000K (roughly)? Somewhere from straight down? Am I the only one to think of earth's core here?

Otherwise: Weird for weird's sake. In a good way. And: EEEEWWW!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The turtle plus the degenerative brain disorder plus the implication that it connects to something like a deep planetary core is making my Terry-Pratchett-sense tingle…

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Well-written, classic body horror. Bit of a series 1 feel. +1

I could've sworn that I've seen this before, though. Did you post this for critique a few months ago, perhaps?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Oh god, it's an extradimensional Grell!

Seriously though, this was creepy as hell and the exploration logs were very good. I instantly recognized the turtle throat, but honestly that doesn't make it less unsettling to see.

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Nope, I mean he's probably a pretty cool dude.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Yeah definitely not, but depending on location, a tumor that size is very likely to cause migraines, behavioral changes, cognitive impairment and/or neurological disorders.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Never heard of him, eh?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: No disagreement there, but brain tumors that size actually do occur- it's not something that would be busting out of the skull, which is what they seem to be suggesting.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This is similar to the cause of Kranztberg syndrome (which is also described as being confused with the diseases listed here) in The Laundry Files, being the feeding processes of extradimensional brain eaters (and is also coincidentally similar to a GURPS monster from "More Psionic Threats" in Pyramid #3/80: Fantasy Threats — by a guy who I imagine is the sexiest, most brilliant, most practically Batman guy in the universe, probably, but I've never heard of him — which is just the mouthparts of a vast elder thing being poked into three space) but psul's characteristic excellent execution brings a visceral enough sense of body horror that more than makes up for any lack of originality in the base concept.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: A 5cm keratin tumor isn't likely to be doing your brain any favors even if it wasn't eating it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Still huge in proportion to the brain.

Not really. 5 cm is a little bigger than a golf ball.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Still huge in proportion to the brain. So a good portion of the brain's gone but how's that beak gonna look with the skull still there? I need a drawing.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
AAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAA.

Now that that's done with: nice job! Simple, more of a throwback to series 1, a solid piece all around. Nice work.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: No mention of the guy with a broken dishwasher?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
You don't have to worry about it being huge:

The keratinized area … becomes approximately 5cm across

Nice and small - you could have one inside your brain now, and never know it :)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I haven't been this disturbed in a while. Thankfully I'm having trouble picturing a huge beak eating brains with the skull intact.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
the picture is from the interior throat of a turtle

This makes it ten times worse.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Windhoek was selected as part of the "fill up the map" challenge - South West Africa was relatively anomaly-free.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
the picture is from the interior throat of a turtle

Yup. I'm not sure I imagine whatever is causing this to be turtle-like, so you can envisage whatever you wish, but good spotting on the image.

Once I decided there would be a mouth inside peoples' brains, I went looking for the weirdest animal mouths. I strongly encourage everyone to look for pictures of the inside of the mouth of a leatherback sea turtle :) There are some terrific pictures, and if anyone finds a better one (eg one looking down the throat) which fits the image policy here, I would welcome the chance to upgrade this.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Actually, my intention was to suggest that there are other areas of space (in Italy, in Wisconsin) which create similar effects. Some poor Italian cow might have passed through one on the way to the slaughterhouse.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This reminds me of the fella who lost his nose and his cousin who got stuck in the oven.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Oh man, is this a cephalopod? Please let it be a cephalopod

ETA: Oh, the picture is from the interior throat of a turtle — still cool, but man I was really hoping for brain-eating squid.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Wrong type of beak ;)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Not calling this article "Bird Brain" was a tactical error

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
psul no offence but what the fuck

+1 this is the good stuff right here

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Goats. I don't actually know if Namibia exports goats to Italy, but that's the most likely one.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
"The world is so full of a number of things/
I'm sure that we all should be happy as kings."
Robert Louis Stevenson had no idea what he was talking about.

The microscopic examination of the Land Beyond the Beak was enthralling. Knowing that the D-class was conscious during the procedure was lagniappe.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: "Unnecessary brain tissue was removed…" +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Testing discontinued? Back in my day the foundation woulda shrunk a MTF and sent them down there!

Also how does meat from nambia end up in Italy. I'm sure it can but it's quite a stretch. (Just ignoring the fact that a beak in your brain isn't)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Although I was born and raised in Windhoek, I've thankfully evaded this monstrosity.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I semi-agree with Roget. Although I actually really love the whole wtf?!?!-ery of the last log involving the heated nickel, I feel like if you had ended with the first log, you could have easily made this extremely short with just as much impact. And I love incredibly short, powerful articles.

That being said, that's largely just a nitpick: The premise is fantastic, the writing is exquisitely well-polished, and the delivery is delightfully disturbing.

ETA: For reference, you had my +1 instantly at "the beak slowly ingests brain tissue".

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I…holy shit. I don't have any words. I literally don't have words. Not many things get a visceral horrified reaction from me, but this? This most certainly did. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Well this is different.

Disagreeing with Roget on cutting the other two logs (and noting that I think he meant the first one was the strongest one). Log 1 does set the stage, but log 2 establishes that it's not human exclusive and 3 ups the weirdness in an interesting way that leaves me with a lot of questions about this (these?) critter's biology…

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The main description is great, perfectly striking a balance between the bizarre and logic. The second test log is the strongest one, the line " Mr Miller had suffered a stroke, presumed to be caused by the sudden appearance of the camera within his brain." was my upvote moment. I think the conclusion was a little weak, I think you could cut the other logs without losing much and making the really good one even stronger.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
What in the fuck.

Just thoroughly unsettling, good body horror, good exploration giving it more dimension. WHY BRAIN BEAKS WHY
